conversationHistory.content:
I will demonstrate how Binary Search works by repeatedly dividing the search space in half to find a target value in a sorted array.
Initialization & Range Setup: Binary search begins with a **sorted array**. We initialize two pointers: `low` at the start (index 0) and `high` at the end of the array. This defines our initial search window.
Finding the Midpoint: In each step, we calculate the middle index `mid = floor((low + high) / 2)`. We then compare the value at `arr[mid]` with our `target` value.
Halving the Search Space: If `arr[mid]` is smaller than the target, we know the target must be in the right half, so we move `low` to `mid + 1`. If it's larger, we move `high` to `mid - 1`. If they are equal, we've found our target!
Complexity & Efficiency: Because we discard half of the remaining elements in every step, the algorithm runs in **O(log N)** time, making it incredibly efficient for large datasets compared to a linear search.

fullResponse.name: Binary Search Algorithm
fullResponse.chatResponse: I will demonstrate how Binary Search works by repeatedly dividing the search space in half to find a target value in a sorted array.

steps.title: Initialization & Range Setup
steps.content: Binary search begins with a **sorted array**. We initialize two pointers: `low` at the start (index 0) and `high` at the end of the array. This defines our initial search window.

steps.title: Finding the Midpoint
steps.content: In each step, we calculate the middle index `mid = floor((low + high) / 2)`. We then compare the value at `arr[mid]` with our `target` value.

steps.title: Halving the Search Space
steps.content: If `arr[mid]` is smaller than the target, we know the target must be in the right half, so we move `low` to `mid + 1`. If it's larger, we move `high` to `mid - 1`. If they are equal, we've found our target!

steps.title: Complexity & Efficiency
steps.content: Because we discard half of the remaining elements in every step, the algorithm runs in **O(log N)** time, making it incredibly efficient for large datasets compared to a linear search.

conversationHistory.content: Container with the most water problem

fullResponse.name: Container With Most Water
fullResponse.chatResponse: I'll demonstrate the Two-Pointer technique to solve the Container With Most Water problem efficiently in linear time.

steps.title: Problem Logic
steps.content: The goal is to find two vertical lines that, together with the x-axis, form a container that holds the most water. The area is defined by the distance between the lines (width) multiplied by the height of the shorter line: `Area = width * min(height[left], height[right])`.

steps.title: Two-Pointer Strategy
steps.content: We start with two pointers at the extreme ends of the array. To maximize the area, we need to find a balance between a large width and tall heights. Since moving either pointer inward **always** decreases the width, we only move the pointer that points to the **shorter** line, hoping to find a taller line that compensates for the lost width.

steps.title: Optimization Insight
steps.content: By always moving the shorter pointer, we prune search spaces that definitely cannot yield a larger area than our current maximum. This reduces the search from $O(N^2)$ (checking every pair) to $O(N)$ (a single pass).

conversationHistory.content:
I'll demonstrate how to reverse a singly linked list in-place by redirecting each node's next pointer using a three-pointer iterative approach.
Initialization of Pointers: To reverse the list without losing data, we need three pointers: `prev` (initially `null`), `curr` (initially `head`), and a temporary `next` pointer. `prev` tracks the node that will become the new 'next' for our current node.
Changing Direction: At each node, we first save the `curr.next` reference so we don't lose the rest of the list. Then, we perform the core reversal step: `curr.next = prev`. This flips the arrow to point backwards.
Iterative Progression: After reversing the current link, we move the entire window forward: `prev` becomes the current node, and `curr` moves to the saved `next` node. We repeat this until `curr` is `null`.
Completing the Reversal: Once the loop finishes, the `prev` pointer will be resting on the last node of the original list, which is now the new head of our reversed list. The original head's next pointer now correctly points to `null`.

fullResponse.name: Reversing a Singly Linked List
fullResponse.chatResponse: I'll demonstrate how to reverse a singly linked list in-place by redirecting each node's next pointer using a three-pointer iterative approach.

steps.title: Initialization of Pointers
steps.content: To reverse the list without losing data, we need three pointers: `prev` (initially `null`), `curr` (initially `head`), and a temporary `next` pointer. `prev` tracks the node that will become the new 'next' for our current node.

steps.title: Changing Direction
steps.content: At each node, we first save the `curr.next` reference so we don't lose the rest of the list. Then, we perform the core reversal step: `curr.next = prev`. This flips the arrow to point backwards.

steps.title: Iterative Progression
steps.content: After reversing the current link, we move the entire window forward: `prev` becomes the current node, and `curr` moves to the saved `next` node. We repeat this until `curr` is `null`.

steps.title: Completing the Reversal
steps.content: Once the loop finishes, the `prev` pointer will be resting on the last node of the original list, which is now the new head of our reversed list. The original head's next pointer now correctly points to `null`.

conversationHistory.content: Binary Tree all root to leaf node paths

fullResponse.name: Root-to-Leaf Path Discovery
fullResponse.chatResponse: I'll demonstrate how to find all paths from the root to the leaf nodes using a Depth-First Search (DFS) traversal combined with a backtracking strategy.

steps.title: Depth-First Exploration
steps.content: We use **DFS** to explore the tree. Starting from the root, we travel as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. This ensures we visit every possible path from the top to the bottom.

steps.title: Path Tracking & Backtracking
steps.content: As we descend, we add the current node's value to a `path` list. Once we reach a **leaf node** (a node with no children), we record the completed path. When we move back up to a parent node, we **pop** the last element from our list to explore other branches.

steps.title: Identifying Leaf Nodes
steps.content: A path is only complete when we hit a leaf. In the visualization, you'll see nodes turn green when a leaf is identified and the path is added to our final results array.
